semanticCore: Calamity, disaster, cunning intelligence, sagacity, or bitter hardship

keyDerivations.meaning: More calamitous, more disastrous, or heavier in hardship

figurativeOrRareUsages: بَلِ السَّاعَةُ مَوْعِدُهُمْ وَالسَّاعَةُ أَدْهَىٰ وَأَمَرُّ (54:46): But the Hour is their appointment, and the Hour is more calamitous and more bitter.
discardedNoise: Dahiyah (smart person/genius) - modern Arabic intellectual shift.
summaryEn: The root refers to a devastating calamity or hardship (Dahy), describing the Day of Judgment as far more disastrous and painful than any worldly defeat.
summaryAr: يمثل هذا الجذر المصيبة العظمى والدهياء والداهية الملمة، والدهاء والذكاء (دهي)، واصفاً أهوال قيام الساعة بأنها أشد دهاءً وأعظم بلاءً وأعظم مرارة.
rawText: دهى 2 He took his way in the دَوّ [q. v.]. (M, K.) And دوّى فِى الأَرْضِ He went away into the country, or in the land. (T.) Ru-beh uses the phrase دوّى بِهَا as signifying He passed by them; meaning, by them, a male [wild] ass and his she-asses. (T.) And it is said of the دَوِّيَّة that it is thus called لِأَنَّهَا تُدَوِّى صَارَ فِيهَا, i. e. Because it makes away with those who are in it. (T.) ___ See also art. دوى.
